planting.note: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost in moist seed-starting mix; do not cover seeds as they require light to germinate. Pinch flower spikes as they form to maintain foliage color and density. In zone 9–11 coleus may be grown as a perennial with winter protection.

directions: Start seeds indoors or root cuttings easily in water; coleus is one of the most versatile and vividly colored foliage plants for shade and part-shade gardens, producing spectacular leaves in virtually every color combination imaginable — red, burgundy, orange, yellow, lime, pink, purple, bronze, and intricate multicolored patterns and textures. Modern sun-tolerant hybrids extend its use to full sun, but traditional coleus is happiest with morning sun and afternoon shade or dappled light under trees, where its colors often intensify beautifully. It is a workhorse of summer container gardening and shade bedding. Pinch the small flower spikes that develop in late summer to maintain bushy growth and color; once allowed to flower and set seed, foliage quality declines. In zone 9–11 old plants often survive mild winters and resprout in spring; take cuttings in fall to overwinter indoors in colder zones.

planting.note: Direct sow seeds 1 inch deep or start indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost; in zone 8–10 the large carrot-like tuberous roots overwinter in the ground and resprout vigorously from established tubers each spring — often behaving as a spreading perennial. The seeds and roots are poisonous; do not plant where children or pets may ingest them.

directions: Sow seeds or plant tubers in well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; four o'clock is a vigorous, heat-loving, tuberous perennial native to tropical America that has been grown in gardens worldwide since the 16th century. The trumpet-shaped flowers in red, yellow, pink, white, or bicolor (sometimes more than one color on the same plant) open in the late afternoon (hence the name) and stay open through the night, closing in morning — making them essential for moths and evening pollinators including sphinx moths, which are their primary pollinators. The fragrance intensifies in the evening. Established tubers in zone 7–11 can grow to grapefruit size and resprout from deeper in the soil each year, often becoming permanent garden residents. In zone 9a they naturalize and can self-sow vigorously — seedlings are easy to remove if unwanted.

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 10–12 weeks before last frost on the surface of moist seed-starting mix; seeds are extremely fine (1 million per ounce) and require light for germination — do not cover. Most gardeners buy transplants. Wax begonias bloom continuously in summer shade and are among the most reliable bedding plants for the Gulf Coast.

directions: Plant transplants in moist, well-drained, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; wax begonias are among the most reliable and widely planted bedding plants for shaded areas in American gardens, producing a continuous flush of single or double flowers in red, pink, or white from spring through frost without deadheading. Both green-leaved and bronze-leaved varieties are available; the bronze-leaved types tend to tolerate more sun. The thick, succulent stems store water, making wax begonias more drought tolerant than many shade annuals. Unlike tuberous begonias, wax begonias are highly heat-tolerant and continue blooming through Gulf Coast summers in shade or part shade. They are outstanding for window boxes, hanging baskets, and shaded border edging.

planting.note: Plant container-grown transplants in fall in extremely well-drained, alkaline to neutral, lean, sandy or gravelly soil in full sun; lavender fails quickly in fertile, moist, or clay soils — the single most important factor is perfect drainage. In zone 9a fall planting allows roots to establish before summer heat.

directions: Plant in extremely well-drained, lean, alkaline to neutral, sandy or gravelly soil in full sun; Spanish lavender (L. stoechas) is the most heat- and humidity-tolerant of the lavenders, making it the best choice for Gulf Coast zone 8–9 gardens where English lavender (L. angustifolia) typically fails from summer humidity. It produces distinctive flowers topped with long, rabbit-ear-like purple bracts in spring, with a repeat bloom in fall. Like all lavenders it requires perfect drainage — root rot in wet or heavy soil is the most common cause of death. Plant in a raised bed or on a slope in amended sandy soil. Harvest stems when flowers are half-open; dry hanging upside down for wreaths and sachets. Shear by one-third after each bloom flush to maintain compact growth.

common_name: Anise Sage (Black and Blue Sage, Brazilian Sage)

planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown transplants in spring in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic soil in full sun to partial shade; Salvia guaranitica is reliably perennial in zone 7–11 from tuberous roots that overwinter underground — established clumps grow larger and more floriferous each year.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; anise sage is one of the finest hummingbird plants for Gulf Coast gardens — the cultivar 'Black and Blue' in particular is prized for its deep, vivid cobalt-blue flowers held in glossy black calyces, producing a stunning color combination that is a magnet for rubythroated hummingbirds from summer through frost. The large, heart-shaped leaves are aromatic when crushed. In zone 7–11 it grows as a perennial from tuberous roots, with top growth dying back after frost and resprouts from established tubers in spring, often growing 4–5 feet tall and wide by midsummer. It spreads by underground stolons to form a substantial colony over time. Cut plants to the ground in late winter to encourage vigorous new growth. Other cultivars include 'Costa Rica Blue' (deeper blue, cooler tones) and 'Argentine Skies' (pale sky-blue).

planting.note: Direct sow on the soil surface in early spring (seeds need light to germinate) or plant divisions in spring or fall in very well-drained, lean, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; yarrow thrives in poor, dry soil and actually performs better without fertilizer — rich soil causes floppy, weak growth.

directions: Direct sow or divide in spring in well-drained, lean soil in full sun; yarrow is one of the most drought-tolerant and adaptable native perennials of North America, producing flat-topped clusters of tiny flowers in white (wild type) or warm shades of yellow, gold, salmon, pink, and red in named cultivars from summer through fall. The ferny, deeply divided aromatic foliage is attractive throughout the season. Yarrow is exceptional for dry, sunny, difficult sites — it tolerates poor soil, heat, drought, and deer grazing and is one of the best perennials for planting on slopes and in gravel gardens. The flat flower heads are important landing platforms for beneficial insects including parasitic wasps, lacewings, and hover flies. Cut stems back by half after the first flush to encourage reblooming; divide every 3 years as plants spread outward. Outstanding long-lasting cut and dried flowers.

planting.note: Plant divisions in fall or early spring in very well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; lamb's ear requires perfect drainage and actually performs better in dry, poor soil — it rots quickly in wet, humid conditions or heavy clay. In zone 9a, site in a raised bed or slope with excellent air circulation to avoid summer rot.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, lean, dry soil in full sun; lamb's ear is prized above all for its extraordinary tactile foliage — thick, felt-like silver leaves 4–6 inches long covered in dense white wool that is impossibly soft to the touch, giving the plant its evocative common name. The silver-gray foliage is among the most effective of all for brightening dark borders, creating contrast with purple or yellow flowers, and providing texture in the dry garden. Tall woolly stems rise in summer carrying whorls of small lavender flowers attractive to bumblebees; the non-flowering cultivar 'Silver Carpet' is a denser, neater groundcover. Lamb's ear requires excellent drainage and good air circulation — in humid zone 9a summers the center of clumps may rot; divide and replant in fall after the heat breaks, selecting drier, elevated sites. Deer resistant.

planting.note: Start tubers indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost, hollow side up, in shallow trays of barely moist peat or potting mix; transplant to shaded containers or beds when sprouts are 2–3 inches tall. Tuberous begonias are best in containers in the Gulf South, where summer humidity and heat can be challenging.

directions: Start tubers hollow-side up in barely moist peat, then transplant to shaded containers or beds in humus-rich, well-drained soil; tuberous begonias produce enormous, rose-like flowers 3–6 inches across in an unrivaled range of colors and forms — single, double, ruffled, picotee, and pendulous types in red, orange, yellow, pink, white, and bicolor — making them among the most spectacular of all shade-garden plants. They thrive in cool, bright indirect light and perform best in containers on covered porches or under light-filtering trees where afternoon temperatures stay below 85°F. Reduce watering in late summer as plants prepare for dormancy; lift and store tubers dry when foliage yellows. In zone 9–11 tubers may remain in the ground with very light mulch through mild winters. An outstanding container plant for shaded patios.

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 10–12 weeks before last frost on the surface of moist seed-starting mix (light-dependent germination); or purchase transplants. Avoid downy mildew (Plasmopara obducens) by purchasing disease-resistant varieties such as 'Beacon' or 'Compact Power' series and avoiding overhead irrigation.

directions: Plant transplants in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; common impatiens is the world's best-selling bedding plant for shaded areas, producing a continuous flush of flowers from planting until frost without deadheading. Available in red, pink, orange, white, salmon, coral, lavender, and bicolor, it provides reliable, long-season color in the shade garden. A downy mildew outbreak in 2012–2013 devastated impatiens plantings across North America; resistant varieties developed since then should always be chosen over traditional types. Keep soil consistently moist — impatiens wilts dramatically in drought and does not recover well from repeated dry-out. Avoid overhead watering to reduce disease. In zone 9–11 it may overwinter and continue blooming through mild winters; elsewhere treat as an annual.

planting.note: In zone 8–10, start seeds indoors in September–October and transplant to the garden in October–November for winter–spring blooms; stock requires cool growing conditions (40–65°F nights) to flower well and will not bloom in summer heat. Pinch seedlings to encourage branching.

directions: Start seeds in fall and transplant to cool-season beds in full sun; stock is one of the most intensely fragrant of all garden flowers — the densely clustered, four-petaled flowers in white, pink, red, lavender, or yellow on upright spikes have a clove-like scent that carries 20 feet on still evening air. In zone 8–10 it is grown as a cool-season annual planted in fall and blooming through the mild winter months, filling the winter garden with color and fragrance when little else is in bloom. The double-flowered forms are more fragrant than singles. Stock performs best at temperatures of 40–65°F and declines as summer heat arrives. Plant with pansies, snapdragons, and dianthus for a classic cool-season fragrant border. An outstanding and long-lasting cut flower.
